info.title: Agentic Tools — Tool Registry
info.description:
# Overview

Tool Registry and MCP Gateway Service is part of the Trimble Agentic AI Platform. It catalogues, manages, and proxies access to MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool servers so AI agents can discover and call tools through a single secure gateway.

## Getting Started

The API is protected by OAuth2.0 and requires a valid TID access token in the `Authorization` header. Refer to [TID Authentication Guide](https://developer.trimble.com/docs/authentication) for details.

Required TID scopes:

- `openid` — OpenID Connect scope for user identification
- `tools` — Use the Tool Registry Service

### Authorization model

The Tool Registry enforces two layers of authorization on top of TID authentication:

| Operation | Who is allowed |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Reads** (`GET`) on `/v1/servers`, `/v1/virtual-servers`, `/v1/openapi-servers` | Any caller holding a valid TID access token with the `tools` scope. Results are further filtered by the per-document ACL (`admins` / `members`): admins see the full record, viewers see a redacted `ServerPublicOut` with internal fields omitted, and non-matching callers receive `403 Permission denied` on single-document reads (or a filtered list). |
| **Writes** (`POST`, `PATCH`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) on all registry routes, including `/v1/validate` and `/{id}/validate` | Restricted to **Trimble organization identities** only. The JWT `amr` claim must contain `federated` together with either `okta_trimble` (Trimble employee SSO) or `trimble_automation` (Trimble CI / service-account federation). Any other valid TID token is rejected with `403 Forbidden`. |

This means non-Trimble customers can **discover** every server their identity has ACL access to and can call tools through the gateway, but **registration and lifecycle management** of servers in the Tool Registry is performed exclusively by Trimble-federated identities. Customer-operated servers are registered under `namespace=tenant` by a Trimble operator on the customer's behalf.

### Quick start: register your first MCP server

```http
POST /v1/servers
Authorization: Bearer <token>
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "name": "Trimble Connect MCP Server",
  "description": "Browse and manage Trimble Connect projects, folders and files",
  "origin_url": "https://connect-mcp.example.com",
  "mcp_endpoint": "/mcp",
  "transports": ["streamable-http"],
  "admins": ["users:my-user-id"],
  "viewers": ["accounts:my-account-id"],
  "tags": ["trimble-connect", "projects", "collaboration"]
}
```

```json
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
ETag: W/"1"

{
  "id": "a3f9c1b2-4e8d-4f7c-9b2a-1c3d5e7f8a9b",
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "path": "/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw",
  "gateway_url": "https://mcp.ai.trimble.com",
  "name": "Trimble Connect MCP Server",
  "status": "registered",
  "routing_status": "pending",
  "enabled": true,
  "version": 1
}
```

The `routing_status` starts as `pending` and transitions to `active` once the gateway has picked up the route (typically within a few seconds).

The `path` field is auto-generated and is the MCP gateway route prefix. Once `routing_status` is `active`, AI agents call tools at:

```
POST https://mcp.ai.trimble.com/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw/mcp
```

## How It Works

When you register an MCP server, the Tool Registry creates a gateway route so AI agents can reach your server through the MCP Gateway. The flow is:

1. You register your MCP server via the Tool Registry API.
2. The gateway route is published and the `routing_status` field reflects its readiness.
3. AI agents send MCP requests to the gateway using the server's `path`.
4. The gateway authenticates the caller, enforces scopes, and forwards the request to your upstream MCP server.

### Route propagation

There is a short delay between registering a server and its route being reachable through the gateway. The `routing_status` field tells you where things stand:

| `routing_status` | Meaning |
|-------------------|---------|
| `pending` | Route not yet reachable through the gateway. |
| `active` | Route is reachable. AI agents can call it. |
| `failed` | Route did not become reachable. Re-enable the server or contact support if this persists. |

**Recommendation:** after registration, poll `GET /v1/servers/{id}` until `routing_status` is `active` before sending traffic.

## Namespace

The `namespace` field is a **required, immutable** ownership partition set at registration time. It determines who operates the MCP server and becomes the first segment of the gateway route path.

| Value | Meaning | Use when |
|-------|---------|----------|
| `trimble` | Trimble platform server (requires Trimble org identity) | The MCP server is built and operated by Trimble (internal services). Requires a Trimble organization identity. |
| `tenant` | Customer-operated server | The MCP server is built and operated by a Trimble customer |

**Key behaviours:**

- **Immutable after create** -- cannot be changed via PATCH. To change namespace, delete and re-register.
- **Trimble-only writes** -- all registration and lifecycle operations (POST/PATCH/DELETE across every namespace) require a Trimble organization identity (see "Authorization model" above). A Trimble operator chooses the appropriate namespace when registering on a customer's behalf: `trimble` for Trimble-operated MCP servers and `tenant` for customer-operated ones.
- **Path prefix** -- the namespace becomes the first segment of the auto-generated gateway route: `/trimble/{unique-id}` or `/tenant/{unique-id}`.
- **Default scope derivation** -- when `required_scopes` is empty, the gateway derives a default scope from the path: `mcp:{namespace}:{unique-id}` (e.g. `mcp:trimble:o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw`). This provides namespace-level isolation by default.
- **Virtual servers** use a fixed `vs` namespace internally and do not require a user-supplied namespace.

## Path Structure

Every server gets a **stable, auto-generated path** based on its namespace and a unique identifier derived from the server's UUID.

```
Real server:    /{namespace}/{unique-id}
Virtual server: /vs/{unique-id}
```

| Part | Example | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `namespace` | `trimble` | `trimble` for platform servers, `tenant` for customer-operated servers |
| `unique-id` | `o_nBsr5O98Qx` | Base64url encoding of the server's UUID |

**Example real server paths:**

```
/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw    (Trimble platform server)
/tenant/Bq1mRk3xTuWLp4Nv6CyEHA     (customer-operated server)
```

**Example virtual server path:**

```
/vs/Xy9dMrJkPqAnVwCeH2TbOg
```

The path is **immutable** after registration. To serve traffic under a different path, delete and re-register.

## Registering a Server

### Minimal registration

```http
POST /v1/servers
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "namespace": "tenant",
  "name": "CMS Vista Connector",
  "origin_url": "https://vista-mcp.example.com"
}
```

### Full registration with all options

```http
POST /v1/servers
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "name": "Tekla Structures MCP Server",
  "description": "Query Tekla Structures models, parts, and assemblies via MCP",
  "origin_url": "https://tekla-mcp.example.com",
  "mcp_endpoint": "/mcp",
  "sse_endpoint": "/sse",
  "transports": ["streamable-http", "sse"],
  "admins": ["users:my-user-id"],
  "viewers": ["accounts:my-account-id", "users:*"],
  "tags": ["tekla", "structures", "bim"],
  "required_scopes": ["mcp:trimble:tekla"],
  "token_config": null,
  "icon_url": "https://cdn.example.com/icons/tekla.png",
  "repository": {
    "url": "https://github.com/example-org/tekla-mcp-server",
    "type": "git"
  }
}
```

### Response

```json
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
ETag: W/"1"

{
  "id": "c7e9f2a4-3b5d-4e8f-a1c2-7d8e9f0a1b2c",
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "path": "/trimble/x-n8pEO1T4-oHJjCfK2UAw",
  "gateway_url": "https://mcp.ai.trimble.com",
  "name": "Tekla Structures MCP Server",
  "origin_url": "https://tekla-mcp.example.com",
  "mcp_endpoint": "/mcp",
  "sse_endpoint": "/sse",
  "transports": ["streamable-http", "sse"],
  "admins": ["users:my-user-id"],
  "viewers": ["accounts:my-account-id", "users:*"],
  "tags": ["tekla", "structures", "bim"],
  "required_scopes": ["mcp:trimble:tekla"],
  "enabled": true,
  "status": "registered",
  "routing_status": "pending",
  "version": 1
}
```

The `routing_status` may already be `"active"` if the gateway picked up the route in time, or `"pending"` if it is still propagating (see [Route propagation](#route-propagation)).

## Updating a Server (PATCH)

PATCH uses **JSON Merge Patch** (`application/merge-patch+json`). Only fields you include are updated.

### Disable routing without deleting

```http
PATCH /v1/servers/c7e9f2a4-3b5d-4e8f-a1c2-7d8e9f0a1b2c
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{ "enabled": false }
```

### Update description and tags

```http
PATCH /v1/servers/c7e9f2a4-3b5d-4e8f-a1c2-7d8e9f0a1b2c
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{
  "description": "Updated: query Tekla Structures models, parts, assemblies, and drawings",
  "tags": ["tekla", "structures", "bim", "drawings"]
}
```

### Mark as deprecated

```http
PATCH /v1/servers/c7e9f2a4-3b5d-4e8f-a1c2-7d8e9f0a1b2c
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{ "status": "deprecated" }
```

The gateway route is removed from the routing layer. There may be a brief propagation delay before the gateway stops serving the route. The server record remains visible in the catalogue.

## Optimistic Concurrency (ETag / If-Match)

The API implements optimistic concurrency per [RFC 7232](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7232) to prevent lost updates.

### How it works

GET, POST, and PATCH responses include an `ETag` header encoding the current `version`:

```
ETag: W/"3"
```

Pass the ETag back on PATCH or DELETE via `If-Match`. The operation succeeds only if the version still matches:

```http
PATCH /v1/servers/c7e9f2a4-...
If-Match: W/"3"
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{ "description": "Only applies if version is still 3" }
```

**Conflict response (another client updated first):**

```
HTTP/1.1 412 Precondition Failed
{ "detail": "Version mismatch: expected 3, found 4" }
```

### Safe-update workflow

```
1. GET  /v1/servers/{id}            ← 200  ETag: W/"5"
2. PATCH /v1/servers/{id}
        If-Match: W/"5"
        { "description": "new" }    ← 200  ETag: W/"6"   ✓

   (if another client updated first)
        If-Match: W/"5"             ← 412                 ✗  re-fetch and retry
```

### Bypass (wildcard)

`If-Match` is **optional**. Omitting the header has the same effect as `If-Match: *`: the update proceeds unconditionally regardless of the current version.

To make the bypass explicit:

```http
PATCH /v1/servers/{id}
If-Match: *
```

Use unconditional updates only when you are certain no conflict is possible.

## Common API Concepts

### Identifiers

| Field | Mutable | Use for |
|-------|---------|---------|
| `id` (UUID) | Never | Storing references, URLs, foreign keys |
| `path` | Never (immutable after create) | MCP gateway route, catalogue lookups |

Store the `id` in external systems. The `path` is a stable gateway route — never changes because it embeds the UUID.

To look up by path: `GET /v1/servers?path=/trimble/o_nBsr5O98Qx...`

### Validated Fields

| Field | Rule |
|-------|------|
| `origin_url` | `http://` or `https://` scheme; **origin only** — no path after the host |
| `mcp_endpoint` | Must start with `/` |
| `sse_endpoint` | Must start with `/` if provided |
| `namespace` | `"trimble"` or `"tenant"` |
| `transports` | Each entry: `"streamable-http"` `"sse"` or `"stdio"` |
| `admins` / `viewers` | CURIE claim strings (see Access Control Model) |

**Valid `origin_url` examples:**

```
✓  https://connect-mcp.example.com
✓  http://localhost:8080
✓  https://mcp.example.com:9000

✗  https://connect-mcp.example.com/mcp   (path not allowed — use mcp_endpoint)
✗  ftp://files.example.com                        (only http/https)
```

### Payload Compression

Supports `gzip` and `br` via `Accept-Encoding`. Only responses > 1 KB are compressed.

## Server Lifecycle

```
                   PATCH enabled=false
                  ┌──────────────────────┐
                  ▼                      │
[registered] ──► [disabled] ──► PATCH enabled=true ──► [registered]
     │
     ├──► PATCH status=deprecated ──► [deprecated]  (gateway route removed)
     │
     └──► PATCH status=deleted ──► [soft-deleted]   (hidden from lists)
```

| State | Gateway route | Appears in lists | Recoverable |
|-------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| `registered` + `enabled: true` | Active (after propagation; check `routing_status`) | Yes | — |
| any + `enabled: false` | Removed | Yes | Re-enable via PATCH |
| `deprecated` | Removed | Yes | Re-enable via PATCH |
| `deleted` | Removed | No (unless `?status=deleted`) | Re-register only |

Soft-delete is the only deletion mechanism. There is no hard-delete endpoint.

**Lifecycle transitions:**

```http
# Disable routing (server still visible in catalogue)
PATCH /v1/servers/{id}   { "enabled": false }

# Re-enable routing
PATCH /v1/servers/{id}   { "enabled": true }

# Deprecate (signal end-of-life; gateway route removed)
PATCH /v1/servers/{id}   { "status": "deprecated" }

# Soft-delete (hidden from default lists; document retained for audit)
PATCH /v1/servers/{id}   { "status": "deleted" }
```

## Data Model

### Field Reference

| Field | Type | Notes |
|-------|------|-------|
| `id` | UUID | Stable, read-only |
| `namespace` | `"trimble"` \| `"tenant"` | Set on create, read-only |
| `path` | string | Auto-generated, read-only |
| `gateway_url` | string | Base URL of the MCP gateway. Clients can construct the full MCP endpoint URL by combining `gateway_url` + `path` + `mcp_endpoint`. |
| `name` | string (1–256) | Display name |
| `description` | string (max 4000) | Long description |
| `origin_url` | string | Origin URL of the upstream MCP server |
| `mcp_endpoint` | string (default `/mcp`) | JSON-RPC path on the upstream |
| `sse_endpoint` | string \| null | SSE path on the upstream |
| `transports` | string[] | `streamable-http` \| `sse` \| `stdio` |
| `admins` | string[] | CURIE claim strings for Admin role |
| `viewers` | string[] | CURIE claim strings for Viewer role |
| `owner_account_id` | string \| null | Account ID of the creator (set on create, read-only) |
| `tags` | string[] | Searchable labels |
| `required_scopes` | string[] | OAuth scopes enforced at the gateway and used for tool discovery auth (see [Authentication and Scopes](#authentication-and-scopes)) |
| `token_config` | object \| null | Credential exchange (see below) |
| `icon_url` | string (URL) \| null | URL to the server icon. Supports external URLs and data URLs (`data:image/...;base64,...`). Data URLs are auto-normalized to WebP 256x256. |
| `repository` | object \| null | Source-code repo reference |
| `enabled` | boolean | Whether the gateway route is active |
| `status` | string | `registered` \| `deprecated` \| `deleted` |
| `routing_status` | string | `pending` \| `active` \| `failed` — whether the server is reachable through the gateway right now. `pending`: not yet reachable; `active`: reachable, agents can call it; `failed`: did not become reachable, re-enable or contact support. |
| `version` | integer | Increments on every write; used for ETag |

### Token Exchange (`token_config`)

`token_config` controls how the broker resolves the credential it forwards to your upstream MCP server. Two providers are supported today:

| `provider` | What the upstream receives |
|------------|---------------------------|
| `"passthrough"` (default) | The caller's Trimble Identity JWT, unchanged. |
| `"token-vault"` *(coming soon)* | A 3rd-party credential (API key, PAT) fetched from Token Vault by `credential_ref`. Falls back to passthrough until Token Vault ships. |

| Field | Default | Description |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| `provider` | `"passthrough"` | Credential source (see table above). |
| `credential_ref` | `""` | Key name in Token Vault (`token-vault` provider only; coming soon). |
| `inject_header` | `"Authorization"` | HTTP header to inject the credential into. |
| `header_format` | `"Bearer {token}"` | `{token}` is replaced with the resolved credential value. |

For most operators, leaving `token_config` as `null` (or `{ "provider": "passthrough" }`) is the right answer — the broker will hand your upstream the caller's Trimble JWT to validate.

### Virtual servers — automatic per-call downscoping

Virtual servers (servers that compose tools from several real backends behind one endpoint) get an additional security layer **automatically**, no configuration required:

> On every `tools/call` against a virtual server, the broker trades the caller's Trimble JWT for a freshly-minted token narrowed to exactly the target source's `required_scopes`, using OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693). Your source upstream receives only the credentials it needs for its own tool call; it never sees the broader scope surface the caller holds.

Concretely, the exchanged token that reaches each source upstream has:

- `scope` = only that source's `required_scopes`
- `aud` = whatever Trimble Identity registered as the resource for those scopes (scope → resource coupling is configured in TID at scope-registration time; the platform does not pin or override it)
- `exp` = short-lived, independent of the caller's original token

Two consequences for callers:

1. **Callers must hold the `mcp` scope** in addition to every source's `required_scopes`. Without `mcp` the gateway rejects the request at the edge (`403 Forbidden`). `mcp` is the caller's consent for the platform to downscope their token per upstream.
2. **The caller must hold the full union** of scopes across every source in the virtual server (see [Virtual server scopes](#virtual-server-scopes--union-of-all-composed-servers) below). Partial scopes are rejected at the edge.

Downscoping for virtual servers is **not per-source configurable** — it is the platform contract. If the broker's token-exchange subsystem is not available in a given environment, virtual-server tool calls fall back to passthrough. Real-server direct calls are unaffected either way: they always go through the `provider` defined in `token_config`.

#### Discovering the scopes a caller needs — from the API response

Callers do not need to open each underlying real server to learn what to put on their token. A virtual-server GET response surfaces this directly:

- `effective_required_scopes` — the **sorted union** your caller must present. This is the authoritative list: `{"mcp"}` ∪ the virtual server's own `required_scopes` (or the path-derived fallback when empty) ∪ every source's `required_scopes`. The gateway enforces exactly this list.
- `sources[i].required_scopes` — the scopes each composed source contributes to the union. Useful for catalogue UIs and for understanding which backend pulls in which scope. This field is **read-only and server-populated** on every create / update / source-change; any value sent in a create or update body is ignored and overwritten.

Example:

```json
{
  "id": "vs-abc123",
  "name": "Construction Suite",
  "path": "/vs/dnMtYWJjMTIz",
  "required_scopes": [],
  "effective_required_scopes": ["connect:read", "mcp", "sketchup:read", "tekla:read"],
  "sources": [
    {
      "server_id": "srv-connect",
      "required_scopes": ["connect:read"],
      "tools": [],
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": true }
    },
    {
      "server_id": "srv-tekla",
      "required_scopes": ["tekla:read"],
      "tools": [],
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": true }
    },
    {
      "server_id": "srv-sketchup",
      "required_scopes": ["sketchup:read"],
      "tools": [],
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": true }
    }
  ]
}
```

The caller flow is: read `effective_required_scopes`, request exactly those on the TID token, and you are guaranteed to pass the gateway's edge check. No need to assemble the union yourself.

**Example — third-party API key via Token Vault (coming soon):**

```json
{
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "name": "Spectrum Construction ERP",
  "origin_url": "https://spectrum-mcp.example.com",
  "token_config": {
    "provider": "token-vault",
    "credential_ref": "spectrum-api-key",
    "inject_header": "X-Spectrum-Token",
    "header_format": "{token}"
  }
}
```

## Virtual Servers

A virtual server presents a **single MCP endpoint** composed on top of one or more real servers (and/or OpenAPI servers). AI agents see one server; each tool call is routed to the correct backend transparently.

Virtual servers support two primary patterns. Both use the same `POST /v1/virtual-servers` API; they differ only in how you populate `sources[]` and the per-tool `overrides`.

### Pattern 1 — Product-specific branding on top of a shared backend

Operate a single backend MCP server (for example, a Knowledge Base service) and expose multiple per-product virtual servers on top of it. Each virtual server renames tools, rewrites descriptions, and tightens the tool's input schema so that from the agent's perspective it is talking to a purpose-built product tool — even though the same backend serves all of them.

Canonical use case: one **Knowledge Base MCP server** fronted by a CMS Vista virtual server, a Spectrum virtual server, and an MEP virtual server. Each virtual server locks the `libraryIds` argument to the product's own knowledge library, renames `search_documents` to `search_vista_docs` / `search_spectrum_docs` / `search_mep_docs`, and rewrites the description for the product's domain. Agents see three distinct, sharply-scoped tools; you maintain one backend.

### Pattern 2 — Aggregating multiple backends into one endpoint

Compose tools from several real MCP servers (and/or OpenAPI servers) into a single virtual MCP endpoint. Agents get a unified catalogue while the gateway routes each `tools/call` to the correct backend automatically.

Canonical use case: a **Construction Suite** virtual server that exposes Trimble Connect project browsing, Tekla Structures model queries, and SketchUp export tools behind one endpoint. The agent sees one MCP server; each call lands on the right backend.

### When to use virtual servers

| Scenario | Real servers | Virtual server | Pattern |
|----------|-------------|----------------|---------|
| Agent accesses Trimble Connect only | Use Trimble Connect server directly | — | — |
| Single Knowledge Base backend, product-specific tools for Vista / Spectrum / MEP | One KB MCP server | One virtual server per product with tool overrides | 1 |
| Agent needs Trimble Connect + Tekla Structures + SketchUp behind one endpoint | Each registered separately | Aggregate into one virtual server | 2 |
| Public-facing "all construction tools" surface, with only safe tools exposed | Multiple real servers | Virtual server with `tool_defaults.allowed=false` and an explicit allow-list | 2 |

### Registration example — Pattern 2 (aggregation)

Compose Trimble Connect and Tekla Structures into one endpoint. For Tekla Structures we only want to expose three read-only tools; the rest are blocked via `tool_defaults.allowed: false` + an explicit allow-list.

```http
POST /v1/virtual-servers
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Construction Suite",
  "description": "Unified endpoint: Trimble Connect projects + Tekla Structures queries",
  "admins": ["users:my-user-id"],
  "viewers": ["accounts:my-account-id"],
  "tags": ["composite", "construction"],
  "required_scopes": ["mcp:vs:construction-suite"],
  "sources": [
    {
      "server_id": "a3f9c1b2-4e8d-4f7c-9b2a-1c3d5e7f8a9b"
    },
    {
      "server_id": "c7e9f2a4-3b5d-4e8f-a1c2-7d8e9f0a1b2c",
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": false },
      "tools": [
        { "name": "list_model_parts", "allowed": true },
        { "name": "get_assembly_details", "allowed": true },
        { "name": "export_drawing", "allowed": true }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

- First source (Trimble Connect): no `tools` or `tool_defaults` -- defaults to all tools included.
- Second source (Tekla Structures): `tool_defaults.allowed: false` blocks all tools except the three explicitly listed with `allowed: true`.

> **Note on `required_scopes`.** The `["mcp:vs:construction-suite"]` value you set above is your own virtual-server-level scope. At runtime the gateway AND-checks the **union** of this list plus every source server's `required_scopes` plus `mcp`. So if the Trimble Connect source requires `connect:read` and the Tekla Structures source requires `tekla:read`, a caller needs `mcp connect:read tekla:read mcp:vs:construction-suite` on their TID token. See [Virtual server scopes — union of all composed servers](#virtual-server-scopes--union-of-all-composed-servers).

### Registration example — Pattern 1 (product branding over one backend)

You have one Knowledge Base MCP server (`server_id: "kb-abc123"`) that exposes a generic `search_documents(query, libraryIds)` tool. Create one virtual server per product that pins `libraryIds` to that product's library and renames the tool for agent clarity.

**Vista-branded virtual server:**

```http
POST /v1/virtual-servers
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "CMS Vista Knowledge",
  "description": "Search CMS Vista product documentation",
  "tags": ["vista", "knowledge-base"],
  "required_scopes": ["mcp:vs:vista-kb"],
  "sources": [
    {
      "server_id": "kb-abc123",
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": false },
      "tools": [
        {
          "name": "search_documents",
          "allowed": true,
          "overrides": {
            "name": "search_vista_docs",
            "description": "Search CMS Vista product documentation, release notes, and knowledge articles.",
            "input_schema": {
              "properties": {
                "libraryIds": {
                  "items": { "type": "string", "enum": ["lib-vista-prod"] },
                  "minItems": 1,
                  "maxItems": 1,
                  "default": ["lib-vista-prod"]
                }
              },
              "required": ["query", "libraryIds"]
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

**Spectrum-branded virtual server:** identical shape, but the override sets `name: "search_spectrum_docs"`, locks `libraryIds` to `lib-spectrum-prod`, uses the description "Search Spectrum Construction ERP documentation, release notes, and knowledge articles.", and tags with `["spectrum", "knowledge-base"]`.

**MEP-branded virtual server:** same pattern, `name: "search_mep_docs"`, locked to `lib-mep-prod`, description "Search Trimble MEP (Stabicad / PipeDesigner) documentation, release notes, and knowledge articles.", tagged `["mep", "knowledge-base"]`.

The result: three distinct virtual-server routes (`/vs/<vista-id>`, `/vs/<spectrum-id>`, `/vs/<mep-id>`), each exposing a single purpose-built tool (`search_vista_docs`, `search_spectrum_docs`, `search_mep_docs`) to agents. The agent cannot accidentally call into the wrong product's library — the `enum` constraint on `libraryIds` plus the upstream-enforced default makes cross-product search impossible from this endpoint. One backend MCP server keeps the operational surface area small.

> **How renaming works.** The tool entry's outer `name` (`"search_documents"`) always matches the upstream tool name — that is how the broker finds the tool on the Knowledge Base backend. `overrides.name` is the alias agents see in `tools/list` and use on `tools/call`. The broker transparently maps the alias back to the upstream name when routing the call.

### Response

```json
HTTP/1.1 201 Created

{
  "id": "f8a2b1c4-9e3d-4f5a-b2c1-8d9e0f1a2b3c",
  "path": "/vs/Xy9dMrJkPqAnVwCeH2TbOg",
  "gateway_url": "https://mcp.ai.trimble.com",
  "name": "Construction Suite",
  "sources": [
    { "server_id": "a3f9c1b2-...", "tool_defaults": {"allowed": true}, "tools": [] },
    {
      "server_id": "c7e9f2a4-...",
      "tool_defaults": {"allowed": false},
      "tools": [
        {"name": "list_model_parts", "allowed": true},
        {"name": "get_assembly_details", "allowed": true},
        {"name": "export_drawing", "allowed": true}
      ]
    }
  ],
  "status": "registered",
  "enabled": true,
  "version": 1
}
```

AI agents call the virtual server at:

```
POST https://mcp.ai.trimble.com/vs/Xy9dMrJkPqAnVwCeH2TbOg/mcp
```

### Tool configuration

Each source in a virtual server supports per-tool configuration through the `tools` array and `tool_defaults` object.

#### `tool_defaults`

Controls the default behavior for tools **not** explicitly listed in the `tools` array:

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| `allowed` | boolean | `true` | When `true`, unlisted tools are included. When `false`, only tools explicitly listed with `allowed: true` are exposed. |

#### `tools` array

Each entry configures an individual tool:

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| `name` | string | required | Tool name as reported by the upstream MCP server |
| `allowed` | boolean | `true` | Whether this tool is exposed through the virtual server |
| `overrides` | object or null | `null` | Optional description and input schema overrides |

#### `overrides`

When provided on a tool entry, overrides modify the tool's metadata as seen by AI agents via `tools/list`:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | string (1–64) or null | Renames the tool for agents. The tool entry's outer `name` is still matched against the upstream tool name; only the agent-facing alias changes. Essential for Pattern 1 (product branding). |
| `description` | string or null | Replaces the upstream tool description entirely when set |
| `input_schema` | object or null | JSON Schema fragment deep-merged into the upstream tool's `inputSchema`. Nested dicts are merged recursively; leaf values overwrite. Must be a valid JSON Schema (Draft-7). |

At least one of `name`, `description`, or `input_schema` must be provided when `overrides` is set.

#### Tool override examples

**Override description only:**

```json
{
  "server_id": "...",
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "search_documents",
      "allowed": true,
      "overrides": {
        "description": "Search project documentation in the engineering library"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Override input schema (constrain parameters):**

```json
{
  "server_id": "...",
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "Search",
      "allowed": true,
      "overrides": {
        "input_schema": {
          "properties": {
            "libraryIds": {
              "items": { "type": "string", "enum": ["lib-12345"] },
              "minItems": 1,
              "maxItems": 1
            }
          },
          "required": ["query", "libraryIds"]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

The `input_schema` fragment is **deep-merged** into the upstream tool's `inputSchema`. In this example, the `libraryIds` property gains `enum`, `minItems`, and `maxItems` constraints while the rest of the schema (other properties, types) remains unchanged.

**Both description and input schema:**

```json
{
  "server_id": "...",
  "tool_defaults": { "allowed": false },
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "Search",
      "allowed": true,
      "overrides": {
        "description": "Search the product catalog",
        "input_schema": {
          "properties": {
            "category": { "default": "electronics" }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Source limit

A virtual server may reference up to **10 sources**. The cap is enforced both by request-body validation (HTTP 422 from Pydantic) and by the gateway (`-32005`). Per-source **tool override arrays are capped at 50 entries**. Split large aggregations into multiple virtual servers.

Contact the Trimble AI Platform team if you need the source cap raised.

### How virtual servers work for AI agents

An AI agent calling a virtual server sees a **single MCP endpoint** that behaves like any other server:

- `tools/list` returns the **merged tool catalogue** from all sources. Sources are discovered **in parallel** for low latency. Tools are listed in source order. Tool overrides (description, input schema) are applied before returning results.
- `tools/call` routes the call to the correct source server automatically.

If a source server is temporarily unreachable, its tools are omitted from the list and the remaining sources are still returned.

### Tool name collision

If two sources export a tool named `search`, the first source in the list wins. The second source's `search` tool is silently shadowed. Use explicit tool filtering — or `overrides.name` — to avoid this:

```json
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "server_id": "...",
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": false },
      "tools": [{ "name": "connect_search", "allowed": true }]
    },
    {
      "server_id": "...",
      "tool_defaults": { "allowed": false },
      "tools": [{ "name": "tekla_search", "allowed": true }]
    }
  ]
}
```

## OpenAPI Servers (Preview)

> **This feature is in Preview.** Breaking changes may still land but will be called out in release notes. Endpoints are tagged `(Preview)` in the OpenAPI spec. Production stage traffic is supported; production environment enablement is controlled by the Platform team.

An **OpenAPI server** exposes a REST API — described by a standard OpenAPI 3.0 or 3.1 document — as an MCP tool server. You give the registry your spec file; it auto-generates one MCP tool per operation with the correct `inputSchema`, and AI agents can call them through the same gateway URL as any other server. No server-side code needed.

### When to use an OpenAPI server

| Scenario | Use |
|----------|-----|
| You already have a REST API and want AI agents to call it | OpenAPI server |
| You are building a new MCP-first backend | Real server |
| You want to aggregate tools from several existing MCP servers | Virtual server |

### Quick start

The full lifecycle is **four API calls plus one direct spec upload**. You never implement an MCP protocol handler — the registry does that for you.

**Step 1 — Register the server (metadata only):**

```http
POST /v1/openapi-servers
Authorization: Bearer <token>
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Knowledge Service",
  "description": "Trimble Knowledge Service REST API exposed as MCP tools",
  "base_url": "https://knowledge-api.example.com",
  "tags": ["knowledge"],
  "required_scopes": ["mcp:openapi:knowledge"],
  "admins": ["users:my-user-id"],
  "viewers": ["accounts:my-account-id"]
}
```

```json
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
ETag: W/"1"

{
  "id": "abc-123-def-456",
  "path": "/openapi/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw",
  "gateway_url": "https://mcp.ai.trimble.com",
  "parse_status": "pending",
  "spec_upload_url": "<time-limited upload URL>",
  "tool_count": 0,
  "version": 1
}
```

The `spec_upload_url` is a **time-limited upload URL** (default: 60-minute expiry) returned by the service. Use it once — if it expires, request a new one via `PATCH ...?reupload=true`.

**Step 2 — Upload your OpenAPI spec directly to the upload URL:**

```http
PUT <spec_upload_url>
Content-Type: application/x-yaml

openapi: "3.0.3"
info:
  title: Knowledge Service
  version: "1.2.0"
paths:
  /v1/libraries:
    get:
      operationId: list_libraries
      summary: List knowledge libraries
      # ... full spec
```

Both JSON and YAML are accepted; the service auto-detects. **Do not** send the spec bytes to `/v1/openapi-servers` — they go directly to the `spec_upload_url`.

**Step 3 — Poll until parsing is done:**

```http
GET /v1/openapi-servers/abc-123-def-456
```

```json
{
  "id": "abc-123-def-456",
  "parse_status": "success",
  "tool_count": 19,
  "spec_version": "3.0.3+1.2.0",
  "parsed_at": "2026-04-18T10:30:00Z"
}
```

The typical round trip from upload to `success` is **seconds**, not minutes. Poll at 2–3-second intervals.

> **Response shape notes**
>
> - CRUD responses (`POST`, `GET`, `PATCH`) return a compact summary: `tool_count`, `spec_version`, `parse_status`, and `parsed_at`. They do not embed the full tool catalogue — fetch it from [`GET /v1/openapi-servers/{id}/tools`](#tool-metadata-lookup).
> - Any optional field whose current value is `null` (for example `parse_error`, `parsed_at`, or `spec_upload_url` when it is not present) is omitted from the JSON payload. Treat a missing key the same as `null`.

**Step 4 — Agents call the tools through the gateway:**

```http
POST https://mcp.ai.trimble.com/openapi/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw/mcp
Authorization: Bearer <agent-token>
Content-Type: application/json

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "tools/list", "id": 1 }
```

Agents see a standard MCP server; they never know your upstream is REST. For each tool call, the gateway validates the `required_scopes`, the service translates the call into an HTTP request against your `base_url`, and the response is returned to the agent.

### `parse_status` — what the states mean

Spec parsing is asynchronous. Your client's job is to wait for `parse_status: "success"` before calling tools.

| State | What it means | What you do |
|-------|---------------|-------------|
| `pending` | Server registered, no spec uploaded yet. | Upload your spec to `spec_upload_url`. |
| `queued` | Spec received, parsing not started yet. | Poll. |
| `parsing` | Spec is being parsed into MCP tools. | Poll. |
| `success` | Tools are ready. `tool_count` reflects the final count. | Call tools through the gateway. |
| `failed` | Parsing was rejected. See `parse_error` for the reason. | Fix the spec and re-upload (or `PATCH ?reupload=true`). |

Parses that do not complete within a few minutes transition to `failed` with a descriptive `parse_error`.

### Status fields

An OpenAPI server response contains three status fields plus `enabled`. Check them together to decide whether the server is ready for traffic.

| Field | Values | What it describes |
|-------|--------|-------------------|
| `status` | `registered` \| `deprecated` \| `deleted` | Catalogue lifecycle. Changed via PATCH. |
| `parse_status` | `pending` \| `queued` \| `parsing` \| `success` \| `failed` | Spec parsing progress. |
| `routing_status` | `pending` \| `active` \| `failed` | Whether the server is callable through the gateway right now. |
| `enabled` | `true` \| `false` | Soft switch to disable routing without deleting the server. |

**Readiness rule for clients:** call the server only when `parse_status == "success"` AND `routing_status == "active"`. These two fields move together — `routing_status` is never `active` while a parse is still in flight or has failed.

Typical transitions you will observe:

- Immediately after `POST /v1/openapi-servers`: `parse_status = pending`, `routing_status = pending` (no spec yet).
- After you upload the spec: `parse_status` progresses to `queued` → `parsing` → `success`; shortly after, `routing_status` becomes `active`.
- After a PATCH that triggers a re-parse (changes to `tools`, `tool_defaults`, `tool_filter`, `route_maps`, or `?reupload=true`): `parse_status` returns to `queued` and `routing_status` returns to `pending` until the new spec is parsed.
- If parsing fails: both `parse_status` and `routing_status` become `failed`. Inspect `parse_error` and re-upload or `POST .../refresh`.
- After `PATCH {"status": "deleted"}` or `{"enabled": false}`: the gateway route is removed and `routing_status` is no longer `active`.

#### Happy path

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AwaitingSpec: POST /v1/openapi-servers
    AwaitingSpec: parse_status=pending
    AwaitingSpec: routing_status=pending

    AwaitingSpec --> Parsing: upload spec
    Parsing: parse_status=queued -> parsing
    Parsing: routing_status=pending

    Parsing --> Ready: parse succeeds
    Ready: parse_status=success
    Ready: routing_status=active

    Ready --> Parsing: PATCH reparse / refresh / reupload
    Parsing --> Failed: parse rejected
    Failed: parse_status=failed
    Failed: routing_status=failed
    Failed --> Parsing: fix spec and re-upload / refresh
```

### Spec uploads

- **Time-limited** — each `spec_upload_url` expires after about an hour. If it expires, request a fresh one via `PATCH /v1/openapi-servers/{id}?reupload=true`.
- **Write-only** — the URL accepts a single `PUT` with the spec body. Parsing starts automatically once the upload completes; there is no separate API call to trigger parsing.
- **Versioned** — every successful re-upload increments `spec_blob_version`. Previous versions are retained.
- **Size** — specs must be **≤ 10 MB**, contain **≤ 50 operations**, and produce **≤ 50 MCP tools** after `tool_filter` / `route_maps` are applied. Oversized or over-operation specs fail with `parse_status=failed` and a descriptive `parse_error`. The `tools` override array on a server is also capped at 50 entries. See [Limitations](#limitations-preview) for details.

### Updating spec-related configuration

`PATCH /v1/openapi-servers/{id}` supports all the usual fields (`description`, `tags`, `base_url`, `auth_config`, `admins`, `viewers`, `required_scopes`, `enabled`, `status`) plus four spec-specific ones that **automatically re-parse** the currently-uploaded spec when changed:

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `tool_defaults` | Default allow/deny for operations not listed in `tools[]`. |
| `tools` | Per-tool overrides (see below). |
| `tool_filter` | Bulk include/exclude rules by tag, path, or operationId. |
| `route_maps` | Advanced: map operations to MCP component types (Tool / Resource / ResourceTemplate / Excluded). |

After a PATCH that changes any of the above, `parse_status` transitions back to `queued`; poll until `success`.

### Tool name aliasing and overrides

OpenAPI frameworks (FastAPI, Spring Boot, Django REST) often emit verbose `operationId`s like `list_chunks_v1_libraries__libraryId__documents__documentId__chunks_get`. These consume tokens and are hard for agents to reason about. Use the same `tools[]` structure you already know from virtual servers — now with a `name` override — to give operations friendly aliases and richer metadata:

```http
PATCH /v1/openapi-servers/abc-123-def-456
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{
  "tool_defaults": { "allowed": true },
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "list_libraries_v1_libraries_get",
      "overrides": {
        "name": "list_libraries",
        "description": "List all knowledge libraries in the tenant."
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "list_chunks_v1_libraries__libraryId__documents__documentId__chunks_get",
      "overrides": {
        "name": "get_document_chunks",
        "description": "Fetch text chunks for a document. Keep pageSize small.",
        "input_schema": {
          "properties": { "pageSize": { "default": 10 } }
        }
      }
    },
    { "name": "delete_library_v1_libraries__libraryId__delete", "allowed": false }
  ]
}
```

- **`name`** on the tool entry must match the original `operationId` from the spec.
- **`overrides.name`** renames the tool as agents see it. Parameter names and HTTP routing are unaffected — only the agent-facing identifier changes.
- **`overrides.description`** replaces the OpenAPI `summary`/`description`.
- **`overrides.input_schema`** is deep-merged into the generated JSON Schema. Useful for tightening defaults, adding enums, or clarifying parameter descriptions. It should only **refine** the schema — never add new parameters that don't exist in the spec.
- **`allowed: false`** removes a tool from the agent-visible catalogue.

### Filtering by tags, paths, and operations

For large specs, per-tool entries get unwieldy. `tool_filter` applies bulk rules:

```json
{
  "tool_filter": {
    "include_tags": ["Library Management", "Search"],
    "exclude_operations": ["healthcheck", "get_profile_info"],
    "exclude_paths": ["/v1/search/v2/*"]
  }
}
```

| Field | Effect |
|-------|--------|
| `include_tags` | Only operations with **all** listed tags are exposed. Empty = no tag filter. |
| `exclude_tags` | Any operation tagged with any of these is removed. |
| `include_operations` | Only listed `operationId`s are exposed. |
| `exclude_operations` | Listed `operationId`s are removed. |
| `include_paths` | Only paths matching these globs/regexes are exposed. |
| `exclude_paths` | Paths matching these are removed. |

`tools[].allowed = false` and `tool_filter` are independent — an operation is exposed only if **all** checks allow it.

### Advanced: route mapping (optional)

`route_maps` is an advanced feature for power users who want fine-grained control over which operations become Tools versus MCP Resources / ResourceTemplates, or who want to block whole classes of operations (e.g. all `DELETE`s) with a single rule:

```json
{
  "route_maps": [
    { "methods": ["DELETE"], "mcp_type": "exclude" },
    { "tags": ["Admin"], "mcp_type": "exclude" }
  ]
}
```

| `mcp_type` | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| `tool` | Standard MCP Tool (the default for everything) |
| `resource` | Exposed as an MCP Resource (static URIs) |
| `resource_template` | Exposed as an MCP ResourceTemplate (parameterised URIs) |
| `exclude` | Hidden from agents |

Most consumers will never need `route_maps`; use `tool_filter` and `tools[]` first.

### Re-uploading a new spec version

When your REST API ships a new release, request a new upload URL. You can combine this with config changes in the same request:

```http
PATCH /v1/openapi-servers/abc-123-def-456?reupload=true
Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json

{ "tool_filter": { "exclude_tags": ["internal"] } }
```

```json
{
  "id": "abc-123-def-456",
  "spec_blob_version": 2,
  "spec_upload_url": "<new time-limited upload URL>",
  "parse_status": "queued"
}
```

`PUT` the new spec to `spec_upload_url`; parsing starts automatically once the upload completes. Previous versions are retained.

### Refreshing a stuck or stale parse

If a parse appears stuck, or you just want to force a re-parse of the existing spec without a new upload:

```http
POST /v1/openapi-servers/abc-123-def-456/refresh
```

- Returns **202 Accepted** and moves `parse_status` back to `queued` from `success` or `failed`.
- Returns **409 Conflict** if a parse is already in progress (`queued` or `parsing`). Admins can pass `?force=true` to override.
- Returns **409 Conflict** if no spec has been uploaded yet — upload a spec to `spec_upload_url` first.

### Tool metadata lookup

```http
GET /v1/openapi-servers/{id}/tools
```

Returns the tool catalogue as agents see it: one entry per exposed operation with `name`, `description`, and the JSON Schema for its parameters. The list is empty while `parse_status` is not `success`, and reflects any overrides, filters, and defaults you have applied via PATCH.

This is the only endpoint that returns the full tool list. `GET /v1/openapi-servers/{id}` and the list endpoint deliberately return only a `tool_count` summary so CRUD responses stay small regardless of spec size.

### Lifecycle

```
                PATCH enabled=false
                ┌──────────────────────┐
                ▼                      │
[registered] → [disabled] → PATCH enabled=true → [registered]
     │
     ├─► PATCH status=deprecated  → [deprecated]   (gateway route removed)
     │
     └─► PATCH status=deleted     → [soft-deleted] (gateway route removed; record retained for audit)
```

Same semantics as real / virtual servers. `DELETE /v1/openapi-servers/{id}` is a shortcut that soft-deletes by setting `status=deleted` and returns **204 No Content**.

### Limitations (Preview)

| Area | Current limitation |
|------|-------------------|
| **Spec format** | OpenAPI 3.0.x and 3.1 only. Swagger 2.0 and AsyncAPI are not supported. |
| **Spec size** | Hard cap: **10 MB** raw spec bytes, **50 operations** per spec, **50 MCP tools** after `tool_filter` / `route_maps`, and **50 entries** in the `tools` override array. Oversized uploads fail with `parse_status=failed`. |
| **Per-user / platform quota** | Each Trimble user may own up to **20 non-deleted OpenAPI servers**. The platform-wide cap is environment-sized (dev 140, stage 420, prod 700). Admission beyond either cap returns **HTTP 429**; contact the Trimble AI Platform team to request a raise. See the [Quota & Capacity](#quota--capacity) section for details. |
| **Spec acquisition** | Upload-only. Fetching via a user-supplied URL is deferred to a future release (SSRF hardening required). |
| **Upstream auth** | `auth_config.provider` currently supports `passthrough` (forward caller JWT) and `static` (fixed headers). `token-vault` exchange is recognised but falls back to passthrough. OAuth2 client-credentials grants are not yet supported. |
| **Trimble Identity** | Upstream REST APIs **must** be protected by Trimble Identity. The gateway forwards only TID-validated tokens. APIs behind non-TID auth (API keys, custom HMAC schemes) should use `auth_config.provider=static`. |
| **Content types** | `application/json` request/response bodies are first-class. `multipart/form-data` is supported for file uploads. **Server-sent events, streaming responses, chunked transfer, gRPC, GraphQL, and WebSockets are not supported.** |
| **Response size** | Upstream responses larger than ~100 KB are truncated with a warning appended for the agent. Rely on native pagination (`limit`, `offset`, `cursor`) for large result sets. |
| **Request size** | Agents cannot reliably generate multi-megabyte request bodies due to LLM output token limits. APIs needing large ingest should accept file URLs rather than inline JSON. |
| **MCP protocol features** | `initialize`, `tools/list`, `tools/call`, `ping`, and tool-call `notifications/cancelled` are supported. **Progress notifications and MCP Tasks are not supported** (REST request/response model has no progress signal). |
| **MCP component types** | In the default configuration every operation becomes an MCP `tool`. Mapping to `resource` / `resource_template` requires explicit `route_maps`. |
| **Schema overrides** | `overrides.input_schema` is deep-merged and should only refine the existing schema. Adding parameters that aren't defined in the spec has no effect on HTTP routing and may cause undefined behaviour. |
| **Parse performance** | Typical parses complete within seconds. Parses that do not complete within a few minutes are marked `failed`. |
| **Deployment** | In environments where spec storage has not been provisioned, registration still works but `spec_upload_url` is omitted from the response and tools cannot be called. Contact your operator to enable spec storage. |
| **Stability** | API is in **Preview**. Breaking changes may still land but will be called out in release notes. |

### OpenAPI Server API Summary

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `POST` | `/v1/openapi-servers` | Register a new OpenAPI server and receive a time-limited spec upload URL. |
| `GET` | `/v1/openapi-servers` | List OpenAPI servers (supports `q` / `search` / `path`). |
| `GET` | `/v1/openapi-servers/{id}` | Fetch one OpenAPI server (returns ETag; current `parse_status`). |
| `PATCH` | `/v1/openapi-servers/{id}` | Update config or overrides. Append `?reupload=true` for a new spec upload URL. |
| `DELETE` | `/v1/openapi-servers/{id}` | Soft-delete (`204 No Content`). |
| `POST` | `/v1/openapi-servers/{id}/refresh` | Queue a re-parse. Append `?force=true` (admin-only) to override busy states. |
| `GET` | `/v1/openapi-servers/{id}/tools` | List the currently-parsed MCP tools for the server. |

Every endpoint requires a TID token with the `tools` scope (same as the rest of the Tool Registry). Per-resource ACLs (`admins` / `viewers`) govern read/update/delete access — same model as real and virtual servers.

## Access Control Model

Access control operates at two levels: the **Tool Registry API** (catalogue management) and the **MCP Gateway** (runtime traffic).

### Tool Registry: Authentication

All `/v1/*` endpoints require a Bearer JWT with the `tools` scope:

```
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...
```

The `account_id` claim from the TID JWT token is used for ACL matching. Make sure you have a proper default account selected in [My Profile](https://myprofile.trimble.com).

### Tool Registry: Role-Based Access Control

The access control is based on roles: `Admin` and `Viewer`.

- `Admin` can read, update, change access control (ACL entries), and delete the server.

- `Viewer` can read the server configuration and use it in agent configurations.

Role is a collection of permissions. Following is a full list of permissions supported.

- `servers:read` - View server configuration
- `servers:update` - Update server configuration
- `servers:control` - Change server ACL entries (admins, viewers)
- `servers:delete` - Delete server (soft-delete)

Following table lists permissions that are part of each role

| Role | Permissions |
|------|-------------|
| `Admin` | `servers:read`, `servers:update`, `servers:control`, `servers:delete` |
| `Viewer` | `servers:read` |

Actors are assigned roles via the `admins` and `viewers` fields on each server document using claim strings. Supported claims are:

- `users:<id>` - TID user identity (source: TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `applications:<id>` - TID application identity (source: TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `accounts:<id>` - user's IAM account identifier (source: TID JWT `account_id` claim)

A special `*` value can be used with `users:`, `applications:`, and `accounts:` claim types to refer to any authenticated actor of that type.

For example:

- `users:123456789` refers to the user with TID id `123456789`.
- `applications:*` refers to any authenticated application.
- `accounts:987` refers to any user from IAM account `987`.
- `users:*` refers to any authenticated user (effectively public access).

### Examples of server access control

1. Server visible only to its creator:

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "viewers": []
    }
    ```

2. Server shared with all users in the same account:

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "viewers": ["accounts:acct-456"]
    }
    ```

3. Public server accessible to any authenticated user or application:

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "viewers": ["users:*", "applications:*"]
    }
    ```

4. Server with multiple admins:

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123", "users:456"],
      "viewers": ["accounts:acct-789"]
    }
    ```

5. Server with account-wide admin access and specific viewer:

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["accounts:acct-100"],
      "viewers": ["users:999"]
    }
    ```

The creator of a server is automatically added to the `admins` list if not already present.

### MCP Gateway: Scope Enforcement

The gateway enforces `required_scopes` per route. The caller's token must contain **all** listed scopes.

```json
"required_scopes": ["mcp:trimble:tekla", "tools:write"]
```

A scope of `"*"` or a scope beginning with `"mcp:*"` satisfies any scope check.

If `required_scopes` is empty, the gateway derives a default scope from the path segments automatically.

**Example: gateway scope check**

```
Server registered with: "required_scopes": ["mcp:trimble:tekla"]

Caller token scopes: ["openid", "tools", "mcp:trimble:tekla"]   -> allowed
Caller token scopes: ["openid", "tools"]                         -> 403 Forbidden
Caller token scopes: ["openid", "tools", "mcp:*"]                -> allowed (wildcard)
```

## Authentication and Scopes

This section explains how authentication flows through the platform for MCP server operators registering their servers and for AI agents calling tools at runtime.

### TID scopes and the `tools` scope

All Tool Registry API calls require a TID (Trimble Identity) access token with the **`tools`** scope. When a TID token is issued with the `tools` scope, TID includes the Tool Registry's `client_id` in the token's `aud` (audience) claim. This makes the token a valid credential that the Tool Registry can verify and that upstream MCP servers can validate against TID's JWKS endpoint.

**For MCP server operators:** your upstream MCP backend should validate incoming TID tokens using TID's OIDC discovery endpoint. The `tools` scope in the token confirms the caller has been authorized to use the Tool Registry.

### `required_scopes` for MCP server registration

When you register an MCP server, set `required_scopes` to the OAuth scopes your backend requires callers to hold. This field serves two purposes:

1. **Gateway enforcement** -- the MCP Gateway validates the incoming token against `required_scopes` **before** forwarding any traffic to your backend. If the caller's token lacks the required scopes, the gateway rejects the request with `403 Forbidden`. This protects your backend from unauthorized access at the edge.

2. **Tool discovery auth** -- the Tool Registry uses `required_scopes` when calling your backend during live tool discovery (`GET /v1/servers/{id}/tools`). If the caller's token already contains the required scopes, it is forwarded as-is. If not, the registry performs an on-behalf-of (OBO) token exchange with TID to obtain a token that includes both `tools` and the server's `required_scopes`.

**Example registration with required_scopes:**

```json
{
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "name": "Trimble Connect MCP Server",
  "origin_url": "https://connect-mcp.example.com",
  "mcp_endpoint": "/mcp",
  "required_scopes": ["connect:read"],
  "token_config": { "provider": "passthrough" }
}
```

With this configuration:
- AI agents calling through the gateway must have `connect:read` in their token, or the gateway returns `403`.
- Tool discovery (`GET /v1/servers/{id}/tools`) forwards the caller's token if it has `connect:read`, or exchanges for one that does.
- Your backend receives a valid TID token with the `connect:read` scope and the Tool Registry's `client_id` in the audience.

### Authentication flow summary

```
Tool Registry API (catalogue management):
  Caller token must have: "tools" scope
  Validated by: Tool Registry (JWKS-based JWT verification)

MCP Gateway (runtime tool traffic):
  Caller token must have: all scopes listed in required_scopes
  Validated by: MCP Gateway (before forwarding to backend)

Tool Discovery (GET /v1/servers/{id}/tools):
  For passthrough Trimble-namespace servers:
    If caller token has required_scopes -> forward token to upstream
    If caller token lacks required_scopes -> OBO exchange with TID, then forward
  For `token_config.provider="token-vault"` servers:
    Current behavior falls back to passthrough discovery
    If caller token has required_scopes -> forward token to upstream
    If caller token lacks required_scopes -> OBO exchange with TID, then forward
  For tenant-namespace servers and other non-passthrough configurations:
    No token forwarded during discovery (upstream must handle auth independently)
```

### The `mcp` scope — when and why your callers need it

The `mcp` scope is a special scope tied to the MCP platform itself. It signals that a caller has consented to the MCP Gateway / Broker acting on their behalf when talking to upstream servers — specifically, that the platform is allowed to mint narrowed tokens for individual upstreams via token exchange.

You need to require `mcp` in your token requests in exactly two cases:

1. **You are calling a virtual server.** Virtual-server routes always require `mcp` plus every source server's `required_scopes` (see below).
2. **You are calling any virtual server.** Virtual servers always downscope per source on every tool call (see [Virtual servers — automatic per-call downscoping](#virtual-servers--automatic-per-call-downscoping)). Without `mcp`, the gateway rejects the call with `403` and the exchange never happens. The scope is the caller's consent for the platform to downscope their token per upstream.

You do **not** need `mcp` when calling a real (non-virtual) server's route directly — those requests go through the `token_config.provider` (passthrough or token-vault) and use the classical `required_scopes` contract.

Request `mcp` alongside your usual scopes on the TID token flow:

```
scope=openid tools connect:read mcp
```

### Virtual server scopes — union of all composed servers

A virtual server exposes tools from multiple real backends through a single endpoint. To call it, your token must hold:

```
mcp  +  every scope in every source's required_scopes
```

The gateway AND-checks this union before forwarding. Partial scopes are rejected with `403` at the edge — the request never reaches the broker.

**You do not have to compute this union yourself.** Every virtual-server GET response exposes it directly:

- `effective_required_scopes` — the sorted union to present on your TID token.
- `sources[i].required_scopes` — per-source breakdown, so you can see which backend contributes which scope.

Both fields are server-populated and always in sync with the gateway's enforcement list.

**Worked example.** You have a virtual server composing:

- Source A (Trimble Connect) with `required_scopes: ["connect:read"]`
- Source B (Tekla Structures) with `required_scopes: ["tekla:read"]`

The virtual server's response will include:

```json
"effective_required_scopes": ["connect:read", "mcp", "tekla:read"]
```

Your caller's token must carry **all** of:

```
scope=mcp connect:read tekla:read
```

Missing `mcp` → 403. Missing `connect:read` → 403 (even if the agent only plans to call Tekla Structures tools). Missing `tekla:read` → same.

This "all-or-nothing" consent model is deliberate. It guarantees that once a request reaches a tool invocation, every backend the virtual server can route to has already been authorized by the caller. Partial access — where the agent can see some tools but gets errors on others — is never the observable behaviour.

When the token reaches a tool call, the platform mints (per source, via RFC 8693 Token Exchange) a token narrowed to **only** that source's scopes — the other sources' scopes never leave the broker. You as the source operator see only the credentials needed for your own upstream, even though the caller presented a broader token. This applies to every source in every virtual server and is not per-source configurable.

### Recommendations for MCP server operators

1. **Always set `required_scopes`** if your backend validates incoming tokens. This ensures the gateway rejects unauthorized callers before they reach your server. For **Trimble-namespace servers using `token_config.provider=passthrough`**, this also enables the registry to discover your tools with proper authentication. For **tenant-namespace servers** or servers using a **non-passthrough token provider**, the registry does not forward an `Authorization` header during live tool discovery, so your upstream must handle discovery authentication independently if required.

2. **Use the `tools` scope as a minimum.** Including `tools` in `required_scopes` ensures every caller has been authorized to use the Tool Registry platform. Your backend can validate the token audience against the Tool Registry's `client_id`.

3. **Add application-specific scopes** for fine-grained access control. For example, `["tools", "connect:read"]` requires callers to have both platform access and permission to read Trimble Connect data.

4. **Validate tokens in your backend.** Even though the gateway enforces scopes at the edge, your backend should independently verify the TID JWT signature and audience as a defense-in-depth measure.

5. **If your server is ever composed into a virtual server, its upstream will automatically receive a downscoped token** on each tool call — narrowed to your `required_scopes` with an `aud` that Trimble Identity assigns from its own scope registration. You do not need to configure this; it is the platform contract. Document in your server's readme that callers of the virtual server must hold the `mcp` scope so agent developers know to include it.

### Recommendations for agent / tool callers

1. **Always request `mcp` alongside `tools` when calling any virtual server.** Virtual servers always downscope per source; the gateway enforces `mcp` at the edge. Real-server direct calls do not require `mcp` — their scope contract is whatever the server's `required_scopes` declare.

2. **Read `effective_required_scopes` from the virtual-server response** to learn the exact scopes to present. This field is the authoritative union the gateway enforces; no need to hand-assemble it from underlying sources. The per-source breakdown is also available as `sources[i].required_scopes` for debugging and catalogue UIs.

2. **Check each target server's `required_scopes`** in its catalogue entry and request the union on your TID token flow. For virtual servers, this is automatic — the platform's listing will surface the combined scope requirement.

3. **A `403 Forbidden` from the gateway almost always means a missing scope.** The response body includes the specific scope that was missing so your token-acquisition flow can add it and retry.

## Filtering and Search

`GET /v1/servers` and `GET /v1/virtual-servers` support structured filtering with **FIQL** (via `q`) and **full-text search** (via `search`). The legacy query parameters `tags`, `status`, and `enabled` are removed; express those constraints in FIQL instead.

### FIQL (`q`)

Pass a FIQL expression in `q` to filter on structured fields.

**Comparison operators**

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `==` | Equals |
| `!=` | Not equals |
| `=in=(a,b,c)` | Value in set |
| `=out=(a,b,c)` | Value not in set |
| `=gt=` | Greater than |
| `=lt=` | Less than |
| `=ge=` | Greater than or equal |
| `=le=` | Less than or equal |

**Logical combinators**

- `;` — AND (all clauses must match)
- `,` — OR (any clause may match)

Use parentheses `(` `)` to group expressions and control precedence.

**Examples**

```
# Tags include both ai and analytics, status is registered
GET /v1/servers?q=tags=in=(ai,analytics);status==registered

# Registered or deprecated, enabled
GET /v1/servers?q=(status==registered,status==deprecated);enabled==true
```

### Full-text search (`search`)

`search` runs full-text search across **name**, **description**, and **tags** (separate from FIQL).

**Examples**

```
GET /v1/servers?search=connect

# Combine FIQL filter with full-text search
GET /v1/servers?q=status==registered&search=tekla
```

### Other list parameters

| Parameter | Type | Example | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `path` | string | `?path=/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw` | Exact path lookup |

By default: all non-deleted servers, sorted by path.

## Calling MCP Servers Through the Gateway

Once a server's `routing_status` has transitioned to `active`, AI agents can send MCP requests to the gateway using the server's `path` as the route prefix. Calling a route while `routing_status` is still `pending` may result in a `404` from the gateway.

**Example:** a server registered with `path=/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw` and `mcp_endpoint=/mcp`:

```http
POST https://mcp.ai.trimble.com/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw/mcp
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <agent-token>

{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "tools/list", "id": 1 }
```

The gateway authenticates the caller, verifies the required scopes, and forwards the request to the upstream MCP server at the configured `origin_url` + `mcp_endpoint`.

## Live Tool Discovery

```http
GET /v1/servers/{id}/tools
```

Connects to the upstream MCP server in real time and returns the full tool catalogue. Useful for verifying an upstream is working and previewing what tools it exposes.

For Trimble-namespace passthrough servers, the Tool Registry authenticates to the upstream on the caller's behalf. If the caller's token already contains the server's `required_scopes`, it is forwarded as-is. If the caller lacks the required scopes, the registry performs an on-behalf-of (OBO) token exchange with TID to obtain a token with the correct scopes before calling the upstream. See [Authentication and Scopes](#authentication-and-scopes) for details.

**Response example:**

```json
{
  "server_path": "/trimble/o_nBsr5O98QxoTHsXj-Kaw",
  "count": 3,
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "get_project",
      "description": "Fetch a Trimble Connect project by ID",
      "inputSchema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "projectId": { "type": "string" },
          "includeFolders": { "type": "boolean" },
          "includeMembers": { "type": "boolean" }
        },
        "required": ["projectId"]
      }
    },
    { "name": "search_projects", "description": "Search Trimble Connect projects by name, region, or status" },
    { "name": "list_folder_contents", "description": "List files and folders inside a Trimble Connect folder" }
  ]
}
```

- Timeout: 8 seconds by default (configurable via `MCP_DISCOVERY_TIMEOUT` env var)
- No caching -- results are always live from the upstream
- Returns `502 Bad Gateway` if discovery fails, including when the upstream is unreachable, token exchange fails, the upstream returns a non-2xx HTTP status, or another unexpected upstream/discovery error occurs

## Validation

### Pre-registration validation

Validate a server payload without persisting it. The request body is the same shape as `POST /v1/servers`:

```http
POST /v1/validate
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "namespace": "trimble",
  "origin_url": "ftp://invalid"
}
```

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "error",
      "code": "invalid_origin_url",
      "message": "origin_url must use http:// or https:// scheme",
      "field": "origin_url"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Post-registration live validation

Check schema, upstream reachability, and gateway route status for a registered server:

```http
POST /v1/servers/{id}/validate
```

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "error",
      "code": "upstream_unreachable",
      "message": "Cannot connect to https://connect-mcp.example.com — connection refused",
      "field": "origin_url"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Error Handling

This service has two distinct error formats matching its two interfaces:

- **Tool Registry API** (`/v1/*`) -- RFC 9457 Problem Details (`application/problem+json`)
- **MCP Gateway** (proxied MCP routes) -- JSON-RPC 2.0 error model

### Tool Registry API Errors (RFC 9457)

All `/v1/*` error responses follow [RFC 9457 Problem Details](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9457) and the [Trimble HTTP API Standard](https://developer.trimble.com/docs/api-standard/specification/latest/standard-error-payload).

```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
Content-Type: application/problem+json
X-Request-ID: abc-123-def

{
  "status": 404,
  "type": "tag:ResourceNotFound",
  "title": "Resource Not Found",
  "detail": "Server with id 'a1b2c3d4' not found."
}
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `status` | integer | HTTP status code |
| `type` | string | Machine-readable error type URI (`tag:` scheme, not dereferenceable) |
| `title` | string | Stable human-readable title (does not change between occurrences) |
| `detail` | string | Occurrence-specific explanation |

**Error type reference:**

| Error type | HTTP | Description |
|------------|------|-------------|
| `tag:BadRequest` | 400 | Malformed input or invalid If-Match header |
| `tag:InvalidIfMatchHeader` | 400 | If-Match header value cannot be parsed |
| `tag:InvalidFilter` | 400 | Invalid FIQL syntax, unsupported field, or invalid value format |
| `tag:UnAuthorized` | 401 | Missing or invalid Bearer token |
| `tag:PermissionDenied` | 403 | Caller lacks required scope or ACL permission on the resource |
| `tag:ResourceNotFound` | 404 | Server or virtual server not found |
| `tag:Conflict` | 409 | Duplicate server path on POST |
| `tag:InvalidVersion` | 412 | If-Match ETag version mismatch |
| `tag:UnprocessableContent` | 422 | Request body fails schema validation |
| `tag:InternalServerError` | 500 | Unexpected server error |
| `tag:BadGateway` | 502 | Upstream MCP server unreachable (tool discovery) |
| `tag:ServiceUnavailable` | 503 | Service temporarily unavailable |

**Validation error example (422):**

```json
{
  "status": 422,
  "type": "tag:UnprocessableContent",
  "title": "Unprocessable Content",
  "detail": "1 validation error for body\n  namespace\n    Field required [type=missing, ...]"
}
```

### MCP Gateway Errors (JSON-RPC 2.0)

Errors from the MCP gateway (when proxying MCP traffic) follow the [JSON-RPC 2.0 error response](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#error_object) format as required by the MCP specification:

```json
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "error": {
    "code": -32001,
    "message": "Route not found"
  }
}
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `jsonrpc` | string | Always `"2.0"` |
| `error.code` | integer | Machine-readable error code |
| `error.message` | string | Human-readable error description |

**Gateway error codes:**

| Code | HTTP | Condition |
|------|------|-----------|
| -32600 | 401 | Invalid request: missing or invalid bearer token |
| -32001 | 404 | Route not found: no registered server matches the request path |
| -32004 | 404 | Route not available: server is disabled or deleted |
| -32002 | 403 | Forbidden: caller lacks required scope |
| -32005 | 400 | Source limit exceeded: virtual server has too many sources (split into multiple virtual servers) |
| -32603 | 500/503 | Gateway internal error (transient dependency unavailable or route misconfigured) |

Pre-authentication errors (before a route is resolved) are returned as plain-text HTTP responses since MCP context has not been established:

| Condition | HTTP | Response |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Auth enabled but no signing material configured | 503 | `mcp-gateway: auth enabled but no JWT signing material configured` |
| Missing bearer token | 401 | `unauthorized: missing bearer token` |
| Invalid or expired JWT | 401 | `unauthorized: invalid token` |

### Status Codes and Retry Guidance

| HTTP | Error class | Retry |
|------|-------------|-------|
| 400 | Bad Request | No -- fix the request |
| 401 | Unauthorized | No -- acquire a new token, then retry once |
| 403 | Forbidden | No -- request required scope or ACL access |
| 404 | Not Found | No -- check the resource ID |
| 409 | Conflict | No -- resource conflict |
| 412 | Precondition Failed | No -- re-fetch the resource to get the current version, then retry |
| 422 | Unprocessable Content | No -- fix the request body |
| 429 | Too Many Requests (per-user / platform quota reached) | No -- delete an unused server or contact the Trimble AI Platform team to request an increase |
| 500 | Internal Server Error | Yes -- retry with exponential backoff |
| 502 | Bad Gateway | Yes if idempotent |
| 503 | Service Unavailable | Yes -- retry with exponential backoff (1s, 4s, 16s, cap at 60s) |

### Troubleshooting

Capture the `X-Request-ID` response header from Tool Registry API error responses and provide it to the support team for investigation.

## Virtual Server API Summary

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `POST` | `/v1/virtual-servers` | Create a virtual server |
| `GET` | `/v1/virtual-servers` | List virtual servers |
| `GET` | `/v1/virtual-servers/{id}` | Fetch one virtual server (returns ETag) |
| `PATCH` | `/v1/virtual-servers/{id}` | Update status, `enabled`, sources, ACL |
| `POST` | `/v1/virtual-servers/{id}/validate` | Live validation |

## Quota & Capacity

Server creation is admission-gated against two dimensions, evaluated per server type (real MCP, virtual, OpenAPI):

- **Per-user** — the caller's non-deleted servers (matched by `created_by` CURIE).
- **Per-platform** — the total count of non-deleted servers across the environment.

Soft-deleted servers (`status="deleted"`) do **not** count against either dimension; deleting a server immediately frees its slot.

When either dimension is at its cap, the admission path returns **HTTP 429 Too Many Requests** with an `application/problem+json` body:

```json
{
  "status": 429,
  "type": "tag:UserQuotaReached",
  "title": "User Quota Reached",
  "detail": "User has reached the per-user cap of 20 non-deleted openapi servers (current: 20). Delete an unused server or contact the Trimble AI Platform team to request an increase."
}
```

The `type` field identifies which dimension fired: `tag:UserQuotaReached` for the per-user cap, `tag:PlatformCapacityReached` for the platform cap. No override scope is available — the 429 is terminal for that admission attempt. Users should delete unused servers or contact the Trimble AI Platform team to request a raise. Platform-level 429s always require Platform-team intervention.

  Soft-delete is the only deletion mechanism -- there is no hard-delete endpoint.

Supports If-Match header for optimistic concurrency (HTTP 412 on mismatch).

OpenAPIServerOut.title: OpenAPIServerOut
OpenAPIServerOut.description:
API response model for an OpenAPI server.

Several persisted fields are intentionally stripped from the public
surface via ``Field(exclude=True)``:

- ``members``: internal union of admins + viewers used for ACL
  filtering; never surfaced to callers.
- ``parsed_tools``: the full MCP tool catalogue with per-tool JSON
  Schemas is available via ``GET /v1/openapi-servers/{id}/tools``.
  CRUD responses expose the lightweight summary (``tool_count``,
  ``spec_version``, ``parse_status``, ``parsed_at``) only.
- ``tool_defaults`` / ``tool_filter``: advanced parse-shaping knobs
  kept out of CRUD responses so the default payload stays small.
  They are still persisted, still drive parsing, and can be changed
  via PATCH; the effective post-filter tool set is observable at the
  ``/tools`` endpoint.

The broker reads ``parsed_tools`` directly from MongoDB, so these
exclusions only trim the REST surface. We use ``Field(exclude=True)``
instead of overriding ``model_dump`` because FastAPI's response
serialization path does not always invoke subclass ``model_dump``
overrides, while field-level ``exclude`` is honored everywhere.

OpenAPIServerUpdate.title: OpenAPIServerUpdate

route_maps) trigger an automatic re-parse from the stored spec.

requests_per_minute.title: Requests Per Minute
requests_per_minute.description: Per-server fixed-window cap. 0 means 'unlimited' (skip the check). Anything > 0 is the per-minute ceiling. Bursts up to 2x are possible at window boundaries -- this is a rate cap, not a precise pacer.

RateLimitConfig.title: RateLimitConfig
RateLimitConfig.description:
Per-server rate-limit override (AAIP-2188 / executor §9 T8).

When the OpenAPI server is registered without this field, the
executor's global ``OPENAPI_RATE_LIMIT_DEFAULT_RPM`` applies.
Setting ``requests_per_minute=0`` is the documented "unlimited"
sentinel — used for trusted internal services that need to bypass
the limit entirely.
